name: Reset by Hanging Up and Calling Back to a Different Rep
description: Recognise when the current call has gone unrecoverable — bad rep luck, locked-in refusal, account flagged 'difficult', stale context — and reset cleanly by ending the call and dialing back. Different rep, different judgement, sometimes different policy interpretation. Done well, this is a legitimate tool; done badly, it's spam.

context.when_to_use: Any of: (a) repeated unrecoverable refusal where you've already escalated once, (b) rep is clearly improvising and won't stabilise, (c) rep has flagged the call hostile / 'note on account', (d) call is going past 30+ minutes with no movement, (e) you suspect you got the wrong department but the rep refuses to transfer.
preconditions: Agent has tried at least one escalation and one reframing within the current call.
User has authorised additional call attempts (or per-call discretion).
Agent can end the current call politely without burning future leverage.

principles: Different reps have different latitude, different moods, different training cohorts, and different scripts. The dispersion is real — not folklore.
Calling back is NOT a punishment to the rep. It's resource-rotation. Hang up cleanly without burning the relationship; you might get the same rep back.
Account notes follow the user, not the call. If you behaved well, the next rep often inherits a clean slate. If you escalated to hostility, the next rep starts with a 'handle with care' tag.
Time-of-day matters. Retention teams are often best staffed midday weekdays. Late-night reps often have less authority. Some companies route by region in ways that shift judgement.
Do NOT call back more than 2-3 times on the same goal in the same day. After that, you're harassing — and the system is now optimised against you.
Between calls, change something: route, time, ask phrasing, IVR path. Repeating identical inputs reproduces identical outputs.

phrases.clean_close_current_call: Thanks for the help on this — I'm going to think about my options and may call back. Appreciate your time.
phrases.fresh_call_opener: Hi, I have a question about [topic]. Could you help, or is there a team better suited?
phrases.new_route_request: Could you route me to [retention / loyalty / billing / supervisor] directly? I think that's the best fit for what I'm trying to do.
phrases.ivr_correction: [Internal] On callback, choose [different IVR option] instead of [the option that landed you in the wrong queue].
phrases.honest_re_disclosure: I called earlier today and we couldn't quite get there. I want to take another swing with a fresh look. Here's what I'm trying to accomplish: [restated ask].

tactics.name: Recognise the unrecoverable call
tactics.when: Any of the trigger conditions above.
tactics.script: Internal check: have you escalated once? reframed once? checked for invented policy? If yes and still no movement, the call is unrecoverable from this rep. Continuing wastes leverage.

tactics.name: Close the current call cleanly
tactics.script: Use `clean_close_current_call`. Tone neutral, even warm. No grievance, no threat. Future-you needs the rep's good notes.

tactics.name: Change at least one variable before redial
tactics.script: Pick at least one: IVR path, time of day, framing of the ask, whether you ask for retention immediately, language used. Don't change everything — change deliberately.

tactics.name: Open the new call without leading with the old one
tactics.script: Use `fresh_call_opener`. Treat the new rep as new. Do NOT open with 'the last rep said no' — that primes them to confirm the prior refusal.

tactics.name: Mention prior call only if useful
tactics.when: If the prior call resulted in a partial offer, a promise, or a documented note.
tactics.script: Use `honest_re_disclosure`. Cite confirmation numbers, not grievances. Past-rep behaviour is data; weaponising it makes the new rep defensive.

tactics.name: Route deliberately by IVR
tactics.script: Choose the IVR path that lands you in the team with authority for your ask. Retention is rarely the front menu — often it's 'thinking of cancelling' or 'change plan'.

alternative_interpretations.observation: First rep refused, second rep refused identically.
consider: The refusal is real, written policy — three reps will give the same answer. Stop calling.
Both reps were in the same training cohort and quoted the same script — try a different time of day to hit a different cohort.
Account is flagged in a way every rep can see. Ask the next rep 'is there a note on my account?' before escalating.
Your framing is consistently triggering the same refusal branch — try a different reframing.
Wrong department both times — try a deliberate retention/loyalty/specialist route.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: If the refusal language is identical between reps, treat it as real policy. If it varies, keep trying. One more rotation, not infinite.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Second rep is suddenly much more accommodating than the first.
consider: Second rep has higher authority (retention vs. tier-1).
Second rep is new and unsure of the limit — accept gracefully but get it in writing IMMEDIATELY.
Note on the account asks for retention to make a save offer — first rep was the gate, second was the close.
Time of day or volume changed the rep's calculus.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Accept the better offer with full lifecycle confirmation. Get it in writing before hanging up — re-call risk is asymmetric.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Third call gets the same refusal as one and two.
consider: User has hit a system-level flag that won't move via phone — try written escalation or executive customer service.
Channel-specific limit — try chat, email, or in-person branch.
Regulatory complaint may be the path forward.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Disengage from phone. Report to user with full call history. Suggest written escalation or alternative channel.

boundaries: Do NOT call more than 2-3 times in a day on the same goal — diminishing returns plus harassment risk.
Do NOT lie to the new rep about the prior call. Selective omission is fine; fabrication is not.
Do NOT impersonate a different account holder when calling back.
Do NOT use callback as a punishment ('I'll call until someone gives me what I want'). Use it as a sampling strategy.
success_signals: New rep gives a meaningfully different answer.
New rep transfers to the right team without resistance.
New rep has no negative note on your account.
Resolution achieved within the second or third call.
failure_signals: Multiple reps converge on the same refusal in the same language.
Each callback inherits more 'handle with care' tone — calls are getting harder, not easier.
User patience is exhausted before a path appears.
Account starts hitting rate limits or security holds.

exit_strategy.on_success: Document everything: which call attempt, which rep, which framing succeeded. Adds to the playbook for next time.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Stop. Switch channels (written complaint, executive customer service, regulator if applicable). Report to user with the full call log.
follow_ups: Log call times, rep names, reference numbers, exact refusal wording.
Note what variable changed between attempts and whether the change mattered.

required_user_info: Authority to make multiple call attempts.
Time budget for total resolution effort.
Channels other than phone the user is willing to try.
